summary: DeVault reflects on how society provides a default 'script' for life — school, career, marriage, children, retirement — that most people follow without deliberate choice. He argues that while this scripted life can produce happiness, most people lack the philosophical foundation to explain why they follow it. He notes that encountering people who deviate from the script creates discomfort and reinforces biases. DeVault calls for deliberate questioning of one's life choices and frames this questioning as inherently radical, encouraging readers to live intentionally and practice solidarity with others who make different choices.
key_points: Society provides a default life script that most people follow without conscious deliberation, rewarded with money but not necessarily self-actualization
The scripted approach favors the status quo and existing power structures, creating learned helplessness that discourages social change like unionization
Encountering people who deviate from the script creates cognitive dissonance and can manifest as inadvertent micro-aggressions
Questioning the script is itself a radical act — radicalism begins when someone examines their worldview and decides they want something different
Deliberate choice is the key: even following the script is valid, but only if you can articulate why you chose it
money_quotes: Upon our entry to this mortal coil, we are endowed with self-awareness, agency, and free will. Each of the 8 billion members of this human race represents a unique person, a unique worldview, and a unique agency. Yet, many of us have the same fundamental goals and strive to live the same life.
You were not asked if you wanted it: it was just handed to you on a platter.
The average person lacks the philosophical background which underpins their worldview and lifestyle, and consequently cannot explain why it's 'good', for them or generally.
You understand how to execute your duties as a member of the working class, but can you explain why those duties are important and why you should spend half of your waking life executing them?
This approach to life favors the status quo and preserves existing power structures, which explains in part why it is re-enforced by education and broader social pressures.
A radical, in its non-pejorative usage, is born when someone questions their life and worldview, decides that they want something else, and seeks out others who came to similar conclusions.
However you choose to live, choose it deliberately.
key_insight: The unexamined life script preserves existing power structures through learned helplessness, and the simple act of questioning why you live the way you do is itself a radical and necessary act.
